cards.back: A linked list is a linear collection of data elements whose order is not given by their physical placement in memory.
Linked lists have faster insertion and deletion operations because only one element needs to be updated, rather than shifting all elements.
A singly linked list is a type of linked list where each element points to the next element, but not to the previous one.
Insertion in a singly linked list involves updating the 'next' pointer of the previous node to point to the new node.
Deletion in a singly linked list involves updating the 'next' pointer of the previous node to skip the node to be deleted.
Traversal in a singly linked list involves visiting each node in the list in a specific order, such as from head to tail.

cards.front: 9.3.12 Reverse Traversal in Singly Linked List
cards.back: Reverse traversal in a singly linked list involves visiting each node in the list in the reverse order, such as from tail to head.
Self-referential structures are data structures that contain references to themselves, allowing for complex relationships between nodes.
Common operations on linked lists include insertion, deletion, traversal, and reverse traversal.
